questions.question: Explain the core principle of the MVC design pattern and describe the primary responsibility of each of its three components.
keyPoints: The core principle is the separation of concerns, dividing an application into three interconnected components to separate internal representations of information from the ways it is presented to and accepted from the user.
Model: Responsible for managing the application's data, business logic, and rules.
View: Represents the presentation layer, responsible for displaying data to the user and sending user commands to the Controller.
Controller: Acts as an intermediary, processing user input from the View, interacting with the Model, and selecting a View to render the response.

questions.question: Describe two key benefits of using the MVC pattern for developing a large-scale Java web application.
keyPoints: Improved Maintainability: Separation of concerns allows developers to work on one component (e.g., the View's UI) without affecting or requiring knowledge of the others (e.g., business logic in the Model).
Easier Parallel Development: Different teams can work simultaneously on the Model, View, and Controller components, speeding up the development process.

questions.question: Detail the complete flow of a single HTTP request-response cycle in a Java web application using MVC, starting from the user action in the browser to the final rendered page. Your answer should include the roles of the Front Controller, a specific Controller, the Model, and the View.
keyPoints: A user action (e.g., clicking a link) sends an HTTP request to the server.
The Front Controller (e.g., Spring's DispatcherServlet) intercepts the request and uses a Handler Mapping to determine which specific Controller should handle it.
The designated Controller processes the request. This involves interpreting parameters, invoking business logic methods on the Model (e.g., a Service layer), and receiving back data objects.
The Controller places the resulting data model into a scope (e.g., the request scope) and returns a logical view name to the Front Controller.
The Front Controller resolves the logical view name to a specific View template (e.g., a JSP or Thymeleaf template) using a View Resolver.
The View layer (the template) is rendered. It accesses the data model provided by the Controller and generates the final HTML content.
The Front Controller sends the HTML response back to the user's browser, which renders the new page.
